one_line_takeaway: Decomposing LLM jailbreak defenses into a 2x2 input/output x literal/intent checkpoint taxonomy shows input-literal filters are essentially solved (obfuscation like leetspeak underperforms even a plain harmful request) while output-stage and intent-level checkpoints remain the dominant weak point, and that binary attack-success metrics undercount true vulnerability by roughly 2.3x once partial information leakage (WASR) is counted.
key_finding: Binary ASR across the full study was 22.6% but WASR was 52.7% (2.3x higher), showing partial leakage is the dominant failure mode binary metrics miss. Per-model overall (Table 9/16): Claude Sonnet 4 -- Refusal 37.5%, Binary ASR 15.0%, WASR 42.8%; GPT-5 -- Refusal 26.2%, Binary ASR 25.8%, WASR 55.9%; Gemini 2.5 Pro -- Refusal 22.6%, Binary ASR 26.9%, WASR 59.5% (consistent ranking Claude > GPT-5 > Gemini across all metrics). Per-checkpoint WASR (Table 10): Baseline 7% (GPT-5/Claude) to 21% (Gemini); CP1 (input-literal) 8-22%, essentially flat vs. baseline -- character obfuscation (Leet Speak, Token Splitting) actually scored BELOW baseline for every model (e.g., Gemini baseline 21.4% -> Leet Speak 14.8%), while only semantic Indirect Phrasing beat baseline; CP2 (input-intent framing) jumped to 45-72% WASR; CP3 (output-literal format abstraction) reached 51-87% WASR (GPT-5 peak 87%, Claude lowest at 51%); CP4 (output-intent/inverse framing) reached 78-82% WASR and was Claude's single weakest checkpoint (79-80%) while GPT-5 and Gemini peaked at CP3. Leakage-severity breakdown (Table 13) among non-refusal responses: Majority Leak (level 2) dominates at 57-61% across all three models; Claude has the highest partial-leak share (19%) vs. Gemini's lowest (4%), interpreted as Constitutional AI providing more defense-in-depth once bypassed. Category analysis (Table 14): Cyber Attacks & Hacking is uniformly high-vulnerability (54-67% WASR across models) attributed to dual-use ambiguity; Claude is strongest on Violence & Weapons (27%) and Self-Harm & Violence (34%); GPT-5 is comparatively weak on Sexual & Adult Content (63%, vs. Claude 38% and Gemini 54%).
core_contribution: The paper's own novel contributions (distinct from the cited MASTERKEY, SelfDefend, and Wei et al. work it builds on) are: (1) the Four-Checkpoint Framework, a 2x2 taxonomy crossing processing stage (input vs. output) with detection level (literal vs. intent) into CP1 (input-literal), CP2 (input-intent), CP3 (output-literal), CP4 (output-intent), used to diagnose WHERE a jailbreak defense fails rather than just whether an attack succeeds; (2) 13 checkpoint-targeted evasion techniques (3 for CP1, 4 for CP2, 3 for CP3, 3 for CP4) designed by the authors to isolate each layer; (3) Weighted Attack Success Rate (WASR), a severity-adjusted metric (linear min-max scaling of a 4-level 0-3 classification into weights 0.00/0.33/0.67/1.00) that captures partial information leakage that binary ASR misses; and (4) an empirical evaluation of GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4, and Gemini 2.5 Pro across 3,312 single-turn black-box test cases built from 81 hand-selected prompts (drawn from HarmBench, JailbreakBench, AdvBench, and Do-Not-Answer) x 13 techniques + baseline. This is explicitly a thesis-style paper (referred to internally as 'this thesis') by Hayfa Dhahbi and Kashyap Thimmaraju, TU Berlin.
threat_model: Black-box, single-turn attacker with only standard API/chat access (no weights, gradients, logprobs, or system-prompt manipulation); goal is to induce a target LLM to produce actionable harmful content violating its usage policy. The defender is modeled as a layered pipeline: training-time alignment (RLHF for GPT-5, Constitutional AI for Claude, RL*F for Gemini per the paper's own Table 5) plus runtime input and output filtering. Explicitly out of scope: multi-turn/conversational escalation attacks (e.g., Crescendo), white-box/gradient attacks (e.g., GCG), multimodal attacks, system-prompt extraction, and fine-tuning attacks -- the authors state their black-box single-turn results are therefore a conservative lower bound on vulnerability.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) LLM-judge has 9% measured disagreement with manual labels and may systematically mis-score certain response patterns; (2) single-turn-only evaluation likely understates real-world vulnerability since multi-turn attacks (cited: Crescendo) can achieve higher success; (3) all 13 evasion techniques were manually designed from literature review, not discovered automatically, so the technique set may miss more effective bypasses; (4) evaluated model snapshots (GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4 claude-sonnet-4-20250514, Gemini 2.5 Pro) are dated to January 2026 and may be unreproducible after provider updates; (5) the checkpoint model is explicitly an analytical abstraction, not a verified claim about internal provider architecture -- the paper has no white-box access to confirm the four checkpoints correspond to real internal filtering stages; (6) CP3/CP4 techniques are confounded with CP2-style intent framing (the authors state CP3/CP4 prompts combine output-stage transformation with input-intent framing), so the high CP3/CP4 WASR reflects a cumulative bypass effect rather than isolated output-stage weakness; (7) small base-prompt count (81) with thin per-category coverage (Copyright 3 prompts, Violence & Weapons 4 prompts) limits statistical confidence in category-level conclusions; (8) the LLM judge (Claude Sonnet 4) is simultaneously one of the three models under test, a conflict-of-interest risk the authors flag but do not fully eliminate.

datasets_used: Authors' own curated subset: 81 prompts across 8 unified harm categories, expanded to 3,312 test cases via 13 evasion techniques + baseline
quantitative_results: Overall Binary ASR 22.6% vs. WASR 52.7% (2.3x gap)
Claude Sonnet 4: Refusal 37.5%, Binary ASR 15.0%, WASR 42.8%
Gemini 2.5 Pro: Refusal 22.6%, Binary ASR 26.9%, WASR 59.5%
Checkpoint WASR ranges: Baseline 7-21%, CP1 8-22%, CP2 45-72%, CP3 51-87%, CP4 78-82%
CP1 Leet Speak/Token Splitting WASR below baseline for all 3 models (e.g. Gemini 21.4% -> 14.8%/15.6%)
CP4 techniques Detection Framing (82-87% WASR) and Evidence Analysis (82-91% WASR) most effective overall
Leak severity among non-refusals: Majority Leak 57-61% across models; Partial Leak 19% Claude vs. 4% Gemini vs 8% GPT-5
Cyber Attacks & Hacking category: 54-67% WASR across all 3 models
LLM-judge (Claude Sonnet 4) agreement: 91% vs. 100 manual labels; 76.6% agreement with Claude Haiku 3.5 on 150 responses
Non-determinism check: 91.9% of 309 prompt-technique-model configs stable (spread <=1) across 10 repeated runs (3,090 total judgments)
baselines_compared: Direct/unmodified harmful prompt (the paper's own 'Baseline' condition, not an external system) used as the reference point for measuring each checkpoint technique's marginal WASR increase
Binary ASR (Zou et al./GCG-style metric) used as a point of comparison against the paper's own proposed WASR metric

Contrast with prior evaluation frameworks HarmBench, JailbreakBench, MASTERKEY, and SelfDefend framed qualitatively in Table 1 (research question, metric type, stage/detection-level analysis) -- these are conceptual comparisons of taxonomy coverage, not re-run empirical baselines
